title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
All you need to know about irrevocable beneficiaries!
You have probably heard of a beneficiary in the context of life insurance. It refers to a person or an organisation receiving the payout from any life insurance policy when the insured person meets with an eventuality. You can add multiple beneficiaries to your life insurance policy, for example, a spouse and a child. A contingent beneficiary may be added to the life insurance policy. The contingent beneficiary will receive the benefit if the primary beneficiary meets the eventuality. Here we try to understand the lesser-known phenomenon of the irrevocable beneficiary.
What exactly is an irrevocable beneficiary?
There are 2 types of beneficiaries:
Irrevocable beneficiary: One has complete right to the amount of money paid out from your life insurance policy. You will be unable to change the beneficiary of the policy, even if you intend to do so. The only means to change the beneficiary under the irrevocable clause is to get them to agree to forego their rights for the funds. Replacing a beneficiary in an irrevocable beneficiary arrangement would often involve legal entanglement. If the policy is registered under the Married Women’s Property Act 1882, the wife and the children become the defacto irrevocable beneficiary of the plan. Thus, they have complete access to the funds paid out of life insurance in case of the unfortunate demise of the insured within the policy tenure.
Revocable beneficiary: This type of clause allows the insured to change the beneficiary at any point in time. The beneficiary being replaced does not have a say in such a change. They do not have any legal rights and cannot object to such replacement. This works better while naming a beneficiary such as your spouse. You may divorce your spouse in the future, in which case you may have to change or edit your beneficiary. If you use the irrevocable beneficiary in life insurance in such a scenario, they could continue to have the power to access funds from your life insurance policy. Replacing beneficiaries is a very simple procedure, in these cases, wherein you approach the life insurance companies and place a request for a change in beneficiary.
Need for an irrevocable beneficiary:
An irrevocable beneficiary in life insurance is often used to name their spouse and children in their life insurance policy. Many times, children are named as irrevocable beneficiaries by parents. This is done to ensure that others do not take advantage of their children’s vulnerability. It also works well in a situation where you have named your children irrevocable beneficiaries, divorced the mother of your children, and remarried. In such an event, your children from your earlier marriage will continue to have rights over the fund paid out from the life insurance policy.
Reviewing beneficiary:
It is not very often that people change the beneficiaries in their life insurance policies. However, upon any significant life event such as divorce, remarriage, or death of a named beneficiary, you may have to revisit the declarations in the past.
Upon happening of any major life event as mentioned above, it is suggested that you immediately change the nominations and beneficiary declarations. This will ensure that the claim settlement happens seamlessly. You can approach the life insurance companies and enquire about how to execute the changes.
If you have declared your now divorced ex-spouse as an irrevocable beneficiary, you may have to request them to forfeit their right to funds from the aforesaid insurance. If they fail to comply with your request, you must seek legal recourse. Depending on the merit of the arguments and evidence, the judgement could go either way.
In a nutshell:
An irrevocable beneficiary is quite a tricky aspect of insurance, only if you are sure that they will be the only beneficiaries of the funds from your life insurance. Nominee declarations are important decisions and should be well thought out. Irrespective of what you decide to do, it is important that you always make an informed decision.
